text.title: Microservices Circuit Breaker Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real microservice deployments.

antipattern.symptom: Breaker opens, closes, opens, closes every 30 seconds; downstream never recovers.
antipattern.root-cause: wait_duration_open_ms too short OR sliding window too small; first half-open call hits the still-degraded downstream and reopens immediately.
antipattern.fix: Increase wait_duration_open_ms to >= 10s; increase sliding_window_size; set permitted_half_open_calls=3.

antipattern.symptom: Breaker opens; every caller now receives CallNotPermittedException; HTTP responses become 500.
antipattern.root-cause: Decorator wrapped the call but no fallback function declared.
antipattern.fix: Add `.recover()` / `.fallback(...)` returning cached value, default sentinel, or degraded UX response. Callers see successful (degraded) responses.

antipattern.symptom: Payment gateway slowdown opens the breaker; orders fail for a downstream that's perfectly healthy.
antipattern.root-cause: Same breaker used for >1 downstream service.
antipattern.fix: Name one breaker per (caller, downstream) pair; e.g. `payment-gateway`, `inventory-service`.

antipattern.symptom: Downstream open breaker still receives traffic spikes; manual retry by caller bypasses open state.
antipattern.root-cause: Retry policy wraps the breaker call instead of being composed inside.
antipattern.fix: Compose order: Retry(Breaker(Call)) — retry counts the breaker's CallNotPermitted as a final failure and stops.
